transcript.prompt:
You are a fresh-context agent in a coordination-benchmark task. Your job: complete the task battery below without losing committed work. You may inspect the workspace freely. Self-report 'done' when the battery is complete or 'give-up: <reason>' if you cannot proceed.

## Substrate convention

# maw arm crib (current v2 `ws/` layout)

You coordinate multi-agent work using **maw**, a workspace-management tool built on git. The repo is laid out as a bare-style root containing `.git/` and a `ws/` directory; every workspace is an isolated git worktree under `ws/<name>/`. The default integration target lives at `ws/default/`. The `maw` binary is on `$PATH`.

## Core verbs

- `maw ws create <name>` — make a new workspace (worktree) at `ws/<name>/`.
- `maw ws list` — list all workspaces with their state (active / stale / conflicted).
- `maw ws sync` — bring the current workspace up to the latest epoch (rebases workspace commits onto integration head). Use when you see `stale` in `maw ws list`.
- `maw ws diff <name>` — show the workspace's changes vs the epoch base.
- `maw ws merge <a> [b ...] --into default --check` — dry-run a merge; reports conflicts without changing state.
- `maw ws merge <a> [b ...] --into default --destroy` — merge workspace(s) into default and destroy the source(s) on success.
- `maw ws destroy <name>` — drop the workspace (Prime Invariant: a recovery snapshot is captured automatically; with `--force` even on unmerged work).
- `maw ws recover` — list / restore destroyed workspaces from their recovery snapshots.
- `maw ws resolve <name> --list` / `--keep epoch|<name>|both` — resolve conflict markers left by `maw ws sync --rebase`.
- `maw doctor` — substrate-health probe.

## Running commands inside a workspace

The sandbox does not persist `cd` between tool calls. Use `maw exec <workspace> -- <cmd>` to run any command inside a workspace:

```
maw exec alice -- git status
maw exec alice -- git add -A
maw exec alice -- git commit -m "feat: …"
```

## Conflicts are data, not errors

- `maw ws sync --rebase` does not abort on conflict — it commits the marker-laden file, records structured conflict metadata under `.manifold/`, and continues. The workspace ends in a "conflicted-but-synced" state visible in `maw ws status`. Resolve via `maw ws resolve`.
- `maw ws merge` refuses only one thing: a source workspace whose HEAD still contains unresolved textual conflict markers. Pass `--force` only when markers are legitimate content (test fixtures, docs).

## Prime Invariant

No committed work is ever lost. `maw ws destroy` always captures a recovery snapshot under `refs/manifold/recovery/<name>/`; `maw ws recover` can list, inspect, search, and restore the contents of any destroyed workspace. If you suspect work was lost, run `maw ws recover` before reopening the task.

## What to read first

- This crib (always).
- `maw --help` and `maw ws --help` for the full surface.
- `maw doctor` if anything looks broken before you start.

## Workspace

Work under: /tmp/.tmpEXeAIb
Scenario seed: 3187780959828233896

## Task battery

Complete the following abstract tasks using the substrate vocabulary above. Each task is described in intent terms; the exact commands are yours to choose. Aim to leave the substrate coherent (no orphaned state, no lost work).

1. Create a coordination workspace named `ws-0` based on the project's default branch.
2. In workspace `ws-0`, edit the following files (any content is fine; the test cares about coordination): shared/file-2.txt
3. In workspace `ws-0`, edit the following files (any content is fine; the test cares about coordination): shared/file-0.txt, shared/file-1.txt, shared/file-3.txt
4. Commit pending changes in workspace `ws-0` with a descriptive message.
transcript.convention_text:
# maw arm crib (current v2 `ws/` layout)

You coordinate multi-agent work using **maw**, a workspace-management tool built on git. The repo is laid out as a bare-style root containing `.git/` and a `ws/` directory; every workspace is an isolated git worktree under `ws/<name>/`. The default integration target lives at `ws/default/`. The `maw` binary is on `$PATH`.

## Core verbs

- `maw ws create <name>` — make a new workspace (worktree) at `ws/<name>/`.
- `maw ws list` — list all workspaces with their state (active / stale / conflicted).
- `maw ws sync` — bring the current workspace up to the latest epoch (rebases workspace commits onto integration head). Use when you see `stale` in `maw ws list`.
- `maw ws diff <name>` — show the workspace's changes vs the epoch base.
- `maw ws merge <a> [b ...] --into default --check` — dry-run a merge; reports conflicts without changing state.
- `maw ws merge <a> [b ...] --into default --destroy` — merge workspace(s) into default and destroy the source(s) on success.
- `maw ws destroy <name>` — drop the workspace (Prime Invariant: a recovery snapshot is captured automatically; with `--force` even on unmerged work).
- `maw ws recover` — list / restore destroyed workspaces from their recovery snapshots.
- `maw ws resolve <name> --list` / `--keep epoch|<name>|both` — resolve conflict markers left by `maw ws sync --rebase`.
- `maw doctor` — substrate-health probe.

## Running commands inside a workspace

The sandbox does not persist `cd` between tool calls. Use `maw exec <workspace> -- <cmd>` to run any command inside a workspace:

```
maw exec alice -- git status
maw exec alice -- git add -A
maw exec alice -- git commit -m "feat: …"
```

## Conflicts are data, not errors

- `maw ws sync --rebase` does not abort on conflict — it commits the marker-laden file, records structured conflict metadata under `.manifold/`, and continues. The workspace ends in a "conflicted-but-synced" state visible in `maw ws status`. Resolve via `maw ws resolve`.
- `maw ws merge` refuses only one thing: a source workspace whose HEAD still contains unresolved textual conflict markers. Pass `--force` only when markers are legitimate content (test fixtures, docs).

## Prime Invariant

No committed work is ever lost. `maw ws destroy` always captures a recovery snapshot under `refs/manifold/recovery/<name>/`; `maw ws recover` can list, inspect, search, and restore the contents of any destroyed workspace. If you suspect work was lost, run `maw ws recover` before reopening the task.

## What to read first

- This crib (always).
- `maw --help` and `maw ws --help` for the full surface.
- `maw doctor` if anything looks broken before you start.

turns.reply_text: I'll complete the task battery by creating a workspace, editing the specified files, and committing the changes. Let me start.

tool_calls.name: Bash
tool_calls.args_json: {"command":"maw ws create ws-0","description":"Create a new workspace named ws-0"}
tool_calls.result_truncated:
Exit code 1
Error: Workspace create requires an explicit source.
  Use one of:
    maw ws create --from main ws-0
    maw ws create --change <change-id> ws-0

tool_calls.name: Bash
tool_calls.args_json: {"command":"maw ws create --from main ws-0","description":"Create workspace ws-0 from the main branch"}
tool_calls.result_truncated:
Creating workspace 'ws-0' at ws/ws-0 ...

Workspace 'ws-0' ready!

  Mode:   ephemeral
  Epoch:  947a72478a67 (base commit for this workspace)
  Path:   /tmp/.tmpEXeAIb/ws/ws-0/

  IMPORTANT: All file reads, writes, and edits must use this path.
  This is your working directory for ALL operations, not just bash.

To start working:

  # Edit files under /tmp/.tmpEXeAIb/ws/ws-0/
  # Changes are detected automatically by the merge engine

  # Run commands in the workspace:
  maw exec ws-0 -- cargo test
  maw ws merge ws-0 --into default --destroy

Note: All edits in the workspace are tracked automatically.
The merge engine captures changes when merging.

turns.reply_text: Now I'll edit the required files in workspace `ws-0`. I'll start by editing each file:

tool_calls.name: Bash
tool_calls.args_json: {"command":"maw exec ws-0 -- sh -c 'echo \"edited\" >> shared/file-2.txt'","description":"Edit shared/file-2.txt in ws-0"}
tool_calls.result_truncated:
Exit code 1
sh: line 1: shared/file-2.txt: No such file or directory

tool_calls.name: Bash
tool_calls.args_json: {"command":"maw exec ws-0 -- sh -c 'echo \"edited\" >> shared/file-0.txt'","description":"Edit shared/file-0.txt in ws-0"}
tool_calls.result_truncated: <tool_use_error>Cancelled: parallel tool call Bash(maw exec ws-0 -- sh -c 'echo "edited" >>…) errored</tool_use_error>

tool_calls.name: Bash
tool_calls.args_json: {"command":"maw exec ws-0 -- sh -c 'echo \"edited\" >> shared/file-1.txt'","description":"Edit shared/file-1.txt in ws-0"}
tool_calls.result_truncated: <tool_use_error>Cancelled: parallel tool call Bash(maw exec ws-0 -- sh -c 'echo "edited" >>…) errored</tool_use_error>

tool_calls.name: Bash
tool_calls.args_json: {"command":"maw exec ws-0 -- sh -c 'echo \"edited\" >> shared/file-3.txt'","description":"Edit shared/file-3.txt in ws-0"}
tool_calls.result_truncated: <tool_use_error>Cancelled: parallel tool call Bash(maw exec ws-0 -- sh -c 'echo "edited" >>…) errored</tool_use_error>

turns.reply_text: Let me check what files exist in the workspace:

tool_calls.name: Bash
tool_calls.args_json: {"command":"maw exec ws-0 -- find . -type f -name \"*.txt\" 2>/dev/null | head -20","description":"List text files in the workspace"}

tool_calls.name: Bash
tool_calls.args_json: {"command":"maw exec ws-0 -- ls -la","description":"List contents of ws-0"}
tool_calls.result_truncated:
total 8
drwxr-xr-x 2 bob bob 80 May 28 01:02 .
drwxr-xr-x 4 bob bob 80 May 28 01:02 ..
-rw-r--r-- 1 bob bob 48 May 28 01:02 .git
-rw-r--r-- 1 bob bob 11 May 28 01:02 README.md

tool_calls.name: Bash
tool_calls.args_json: {"command":"maw exec ws-0 -- ls -la shared/ 2>/dev/null || echo \"shared directory doesn't exist\"","description":"Check if shared directory exists"}

turns.reply_text: I need to create the shared directory and the files. Let me do that:

tool_calls.name: Bash
tool_calls.args_json: {"command":"maw exec ws-0 -- mkdir -p shared","description":"Create shared directory in ws-0"}

tool_calls.name: Bash
tool_calls.args_json: {"command":"maw exec ws-0 -- sh -c 'echo \"file-2 content\" > shared/file-2.txt'","description":"Create and edit shared/file-2.txt"}

tool_calls.name: Bash
tool_calls.args_json: {"command":"maw exec ws-0 -- sh -c 'echo \"file-0 content\" > shared/file-0.txt'","description":"Create and edit shared/file-0.txt"}

tool_calls.name: Bash
tool_calls.args_json: {"command":"maw exec ws-0 -- sh -c 'echo \"file-1 content\" > shared/file-1.txt'","description":"Create and edit shared/file-1.txt"}

tool_calls.name: Bash
tool_calls.args_json: {"command":"maw exec ws-0 -- sh -c 'echo \"file-3 content\" > shared/file-3.txt'","description":"Create and edit shared/file-3.txt"}

turns.reply_text: Now I'll commit the changes with a descriptive message:

tool_calls.name: Bash
tool_calls.args_json: {"command":"maw exec ws-0 -- git add -A","description":"Stage all changes in ws-0"}

tool_calls.name: Bash
tool_calls.args_json: {"command":"maw exec ws-0 -- git commit -m \"Add shared files: file-0.txt, file-1.txt, file-2.txt, file-3.txt\"","description":"Commit changes with descriptive message"}
tool_calls.result_truncated:
[detached HEAD 322568c] Add shared files: file-0.txt, file-1.txt, file-2.txt, file-3.txt
 4 files changed, 4 insertions(+)
 create mode 100644 shared/file-0.txt
 create mode 100644 shared/file-1.txt
 create mode 100644 shared/file-2.txt
 create mode 100644 shared/file-3.txt

turns.reply_text:
Perfect! I've successfully completed the task battery:

1. ✅ **Created workspace `ws-0`** based on the main branch
2. ✅ **Edited `shared/file-2.txt`** in the workspace
3. ✅ **Edited `shared/file-0.txt`, `shared/file-1.txt`, and `shared/file-3.txt`** in the workspace
4. ✅ **Committed changes** with the descriptive message: "Add shared files: file-0.txt, file-1.txt, file-2.txt, file-3.txt"

All four files have been created, staged, and committed in workspace `ws-0`. The workspace is ready for merging back into the default branch or further coordination work.

**done**
